summary: Japanese architect (born 1957), 2014 Pritzker Prize winner, known for using paper tubes and recycled materials for humanitarian disaster relief housing. Key buildings include the Cardboard Cathedral in Christchurch, New Zealand and the Centre Pompidou-Metz. Often builds temporary churches and refugee shelters using cardboard tubes after natural disasters.

works.name: Cardboard Cathedral, Christchurch
works.description: Transitional cathedral built for the Anglican Diocese of Christchurch after the 2011 earthquake destroyed the original. Eight shipping containers form the walls; the roof is a triangular polycarbonate structure. A paper tube studio used in the design process was incorporated into the final design. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Transitional cathedral in Christchurch built after the 2011 earthquake; eight shipping containers form the walls; triangular polycarbonate roof
examples: This architect won the 2014 Pritzker Prize for his design of the Cardboard Cathedral in Christchurch.
This architect is best known for constructing a transitional Cathedral partially out of cardboard for the Anglican Diocese of Christchurch after a 2011 earthquake.
Eight shipping containers form the walls of this architect's Cardboard Cathedral in Christchurch.

works.name: Centre Pompidou-Metz (Roof)
works.description: The roof of the Centre Pompidou-Metz in northeastern France, designed by Shigeru Ban. Inspired by a Chinese bamboo hat, it is a hexagonal shell made from glue-laminated timber (or laminated wood). A paper tube studio used to develop the design was incorporated into the final building. The museum also features a glass elevator inspired by a gondola, from which visitors 'ski' down to the galleries. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Roof inspired by a Chinese bamboo hat; hexagonal shell of glue-laminated timber; paper tube studio incorporated into the final design
examples: A bamboo hat inspired the hexagonal shell made from timber he used for the roof of a Metz art museum.
An architect from this country was inspired by a Chinese bamboo hat to use hexagonal units of laminated wood in his design for the roof of the Centre Pompidou-Metz.

A paper tube studio used to design one of these buildings was incorporated into the final design; the design for the roof of that one of these buildings was inspired by a Chinese hat found by Shigeru Ban.

clues.clue: Glass elevator inspired by a gondola; visitors 'ski' down to the galleries
examples: An art museum designed by this man has a glass elevator inspired by a gondola from which patrons then 'ski' down to the galleries.

works.name: Humanitarian / Paper Tube Work
works.description: Shigeru Ban is renowned for using paper tubes as cheap, recyclable building materials after natural disasters. Following the 1995 Kobe earthquake, he built refugee shelters and the Takatori Catholic Church ('Paper Dome') in Kobe (later moved to Taiwan). He also substituted aluminum rods with cardboard tubes for refugee camps in Byumba, Rwanda. He designed an Expo 2000 pavilion (with Frei Otto) whose roof was made entirely of paper. The New York Times critic Michael Kimmelman called him an 'accidental environmentalist.'

clues.clue: Uses paper tubes and recycled/recyclable materials for disaster relief housing and refugee shelters (Kobe earthquake, Rwanda refugee camps)
examples: This man's own summer house on Lake Yamanaka was one of the first to use paper for a building material; he subsequently used paper for his series of refugee houses following the Kobe earthquake.
He has been praised for his creative use of materials to rebuild destroyed housing developments.
During the construction of one of these places [refugee camps] in Rwanda, Shigeru Ban substituted aluminum rods with cardboard tubes.

clues.clue: Collaborated with Frei Otto on the Japanese Pavilion at Expo 2000 in Hanover, with a roof made entirely of paper
examples: Otto collaborated with this architect on a pavilion design for Expo 2000 featuring a roof made entirely of paper.
engineer Frei Otto helped design the Japanese pavilion made out of this material [cardboard paper] for Hanover's Expo 2000.

works.name: Other Works / General
works.description: Won the 2014 Pritzker Prize. Designed transparent toilets for the 2020 Tokyo Olympics whose walls turn opaque when occupied. Designed the Aspen Museum of Art (his first US museum). The Nomadic Museum was designed for photographer Gregory Colbert's Ashes and Snow exhibition. Some structures anchored by sand-filled Kirin beer crates. His summer house on Lake Yamanaka was among the first buildings to use paper tubes.

examples: name this Japanese winner of the 2014 Pritzker Prize and designer of the Cardboard Cathedral.
The 2014 Pritzker Prize went to a 'disaster architect' from this country

clues.clue: Designed transparent glass toilets for the 2020 Tokyo Olympics whose walls turn opaque if occupied
examples: In 2020, an architect from this country unveiled transparent glass toilet stalls whose walls turn opaque if occupied.
'Smart glass' with varying opacity was included in some of these places [toilets] designed by Shigeru Ban for the Covid-delayed Tokyo Olympics.

clues.clue: Designed the Aspen Museum of Art (his first US museum); designed the Nomadic Museum for Gregory Colbert's Ashes and Snow
examples: He designed the Aspen Museum of Art.
This architect designed the sustainable travelling 'Nomadic Museum' for an exhibition by Canadian artist Gregory Colbert titled Ashes and Snow.

clues.clue: Some structures anchored by sand-filled Kirin beer crates
examples: Some of his structures are anchored by sand-filled Kirin beer crates.

comprehensive_summary:
Shigeru Ban (born 1957) is a Japanese architect known for using paper tubes and other recycled or lightweight materials for humanitarian disaster relief housing and temporary structures. He won the 2014 Pritzker Prize.

His most internationally recognized project is the Cardboard Cathedral in Christchurch, New Zealand, built after the 2011 earthquake destroyed the original Anglican cathedral. The transitional building uses eight shipping containers as its walls and a triangular polycarbonate roof.

For the Centre Pompidou-Metz in northeastern France, Ban designed a distinctive hexagonal roof of glue-laminated timber, inspired by the shape of a Chinese bamboo hat. A paper tube studio he used during the design process was incorporated into the final building. The museum also features a glass elevator inspired by a gondola, from which visitors 'ski' down to the galleries.

Ban's humanitarian work is central to his reputation. After the 1995 Kobe earthquake, he designed refugee houses using paper tubes and built the Takatori Catholic Church ('Paper Dome') in Kobe, which was later moved to Taiwan. He substituted aluminum rods with cardboard tubes for refugee camps in Byumba, Rwanda. He collaborated with German architect Frei Otto on the Japanese Pavilion at Expo 2000 in Hanover, which had a roof made entirely of paper.

Other notable works include transparent toilets for the 2020 Tokyo Olympics (whose glass walls turn opaque when occupied), the Aspen Museum of Art (his first US museum, housed in a converted hydroelectric plant), and the Nomadic Museum for photographer Gregory Colbert's Ashes and Snow exhibition. Some of his structures are anchored by sand-filled Kirin beer crates. The New York Times critic Michael Kimmelman called him an 'accidental environmentalist.'
